mappings.rationaleAI: Converts energy forms into mechanical energy, encompassing internal combustion engines (B.8.a), steam engines (B.4.a), and electric motors; generators perform the inverse conversion for electric power generation (C.7).
Gasoline, diesel, gas turbine, and jet engines where fuel combustion occurs within the working-fluid circuit directly correspond to internal-combustion engines (B.8.a).
Converts electrical energy into mechanical energy via magnetic field interaction, with induction, synchronous, and commutator types corresponding to electric motors (B.5.b).
Electrochemical cells converting chemical energy into electrical energy via redox reactions directly correspond to batteries and fuel cells (C.4).
P-n junctions forming diodes, transistors, and integrated circuits correspond to solid-state devices including transistors and semiconductor diodes (D.2).
Design and operation of devices using transistors, diodes, and integrated circuits corresponds to electron tubes (D.1) and solid-state devices (D.2) for energy concentration and control.
Designed the AC induction motor (B.5.b) and polyphase AC power system forming the cornerstone of modern electrical power distribution and generation (C.7).
The earliest chemical explosive consisting of potassium nitrate, charcoal, and sulfur corresponds to black powder as a chemical explosive (B.8.b).
External combustion heat engine using steam pressure to drive pistons or turbines corresponds to steam engines and steam power plants (B.4.a).
Electric charge, current, and electromagnetic induction correspond to devices utilizing magnetic and electrical energy (B.5) and electric power generation (C.7).
Crude oil as a non-renewable hydrocarbon energy source corresponds to nonrenewable energy sources including oil (A.3).

Electricity generation through nuclear fission corresponds to primary energy sources including nuclear fission and radioactivity (A.1) and devices for utilizing nuclear energy including fission reactors (B.9).

Converting gravitational potential energy of flowing water into electricity corresponds to recurring water energy sources (A.2) and devices utilizing water energy including waterwheels and water turbines (B.2).
Ionized gas behavior relevant to nuclear fusion reactors and plasma-based energy applications (B.9).
Silicon as a semiconductor material for transistors, integrated circuits, and solar cells corresponds to solid-state devices (D.2) and photovoltaic devices for power generation (C.7).
Incandescent, gas-discharge, and LED lighting devices converting electrical energy into visible light correspond to lamps and other lighting devices (C.5).
Self-propelled vehicles generating thrust via chemical reaction correspond to rocket engines as internal-combustion engine types (B.8.a).
Produces coherent light through stimulated emission in semiconductor and other gain media, corresponding to optoelectronic devices including semiconductor lasers (D.3).
Reactive substances releasing stored energy correspond to chemical explosives including black powder, nitroglycerin, dynamites, and military explosives (B.8.b).
Fossil fuel composed primarily of methane corresponds to nonrenewable energy sources including natural gas (A.3).
Electromagnetic radiation harnessed via photovoltaic and concentrated solar power corresponds to recurring solar energy sources (A.2) and devices for utilizing solar energy (B.10).
Heat transfer using vapour-compression and thermoelectric effects corresponds to devices for utilizing heat energy including refrigeration equipment (B.7).

The conserved scalar physical quantity representing capacity to do work, manifesting as kinetic, potential, chemical, thermal, radiant, and nuclear forms, corresponds to major types of energy useful to humankind (A).
Coal, petroleum, and natural gas as non-renewable carbon-rich energy sources correspond to nonrenewable energy sources (A.3).
Spontaneous nuclear transformation releasing energy as radiation corresponds to radioactivity as a primary energy source (A.1).
Air movement driven by differential solar heating corresponds to recurring wind energy sources (A.2) and devices utilizing wind energy including windmills (B.2).
Combustible sedimentary rock supplying a quarter of global primary energy corresponds to nonrenewable energy sources including coal (A.3).
The six classical mechanical devices including lever, pulley, and wheel correspond to devices for utilizing muscle energy (B.1).
Barriers creating reservoirs for hydroelectric power generation correspond to devices utilizing water energy (B.2) and hydraulic-turbine-driven generators (C.7).
Free and bound electrons enabling cathode ray tubes and semiconductor devices correspond to electron tubes (D.1) and solid-state devices (D.2).
